coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of intercalated γ-Bi2MoO6/graphene nanosheet composites for high performance NO2 gas sensor at room temperature

Lv, He
Ma, Laifeng
Li, Wenna
Si, Jiaqi
Ikram, Muhammad
Ullah, Mohib
Wu, Hongyuan
Wang, Ruihong
Shi, Keying
coredata.dc:description: Intercalated γ-Bi2MoO6/graphene nanosheet composites (BMO/GNCs) were prepared by vacuum-assistant and solvothermal technique. In BMO/GNCs, BMO nanoparticles (NPs) were uniformly distributed on the surface and interlayer of graphene nanosheets (GNs), simultaneously along with a large number of defects and interface on GNs and BMO NPs. As synthesized with solvothermal temperature at 160 °C and graphite content of 80 mg, BMO/GNCs-8-160 sample exhibits the remarkable gas sensitivity, reversibility and selectivity to NO2 at room temperature (RT, 22 °C). The relative response rate of the sensor toward 100 ppm NO2 is 96.73, which is about 6.41 times of pure BMO NPs. Furthermore, the response time and recovery time is merely 2.5 and 34.1 s, respectively. The detection limit can reach 10 ppb with response value of 1.51. The improved NO2 sensing performance is attributed to the effective electron transfer between BMO NPs and GNs and typical structure characteristics of BMO/GNCs, which is rarely reported in the room temperature type of Bi2MoO6 sensor.

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis of intercalated γ-Bi2MoO6/graphene nanosheet composites for high performance NO2 gas sensor at room temperature

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intercalated γ-Bi2MoO6/graphene nanosheet composites (BMO/GNCs) were prepared by vacuum-assistant and solvothermal technique. In BMO/GNCs, BMO nanoparticles (NPs) were uniformly distributed on the surface and interlayer of graphene nanosheets (GNs), simultaneously along with a large number of defects and interface on GNs and BMO NPs. As synthesized with solvothermal temperature at 160 °C and graphite content of 80 mg, BMO/GNCs-8-160 sample exhibits the remarkable gas sensitivity, reversibility and selectivity to NO2 at room temperature (RT, 22 °C). The relative response rate of the sensor toward 100 ppm NO2 is 96.73, which is about 6.41 times of pure BMO NPs. Furthermore, the response time and recovery time is merely 2.5 and 34.1 s, respectively. The detection limit can reach 10 ppb with response value of 1.51. The improved NO2 sensing performance is attributed to the effective electron transfer between BMO NPs and GNs and typical structure characteristics of BMO/GNCs, which is rarely reported in the room temperature type of Bi2MoO6 sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, a great majority of cities in the world have suffered from serious air pollution [1]. Nitrogen Oxide NOx (NO, NO2) produced by fossil fuels, detergents, vehicle exhaust is considered to be the culprit of air pollution [2], also in that it is the typical harmful gas and has a fatal effect on human health [3], by resulting of a series of diseases such as asthma, eye irritation, fatigue and nausea etc. [4] Transiently exposing to NO2 gas at 10–20 ppm can cause mild stimulation of the nose and throat [5,6]. Even worse, exposing to more than 100 ppm of NO2 gas even can lead to death because of fluid asphyxia in the lungs [7]. Hence, it is vital to proceed real-time, accurate and continuous monitoring of NO2 in the environment. As a typical Aurivillius structure compound, bismuth molybdate is a representative bismuth molybdenum ore oxide. Its general structure is Bi2O3•nMoO3 (n = 3, 2 or 1), which is respectively corresponds to α-Bi2Mo3O12, β-Bi2Mo2O9 and γ-Bi2MoO6 (BMO) [8], BMO, composed of MoO4 2− perovskite lamellae and (Bi2O2)2+ layers, is a layered Aurivillius type oxide (band gap is about 2.65 eV) [9]. The molybdenum oxide layer is a kind of perovskite structure with a shared structure of eight sides, and the angular shared eight surface structure provides more space for the ionization of electrons, making the electronic transmission more conducive to the gas sensor [10–13]. Meanwhile, the electron moves under the angle sharing system, without step barrier, and the transmission rate is faster.

In recent decades, the potential applications of BMO in visible light self-cleaning materials, luminescent materials [14], photocatalyst and humidity sensors have attracted considerable attention [15–18]. (see Table S1). However, for BMO, shortages of low quantum yields, inefficient charge separation and transport, insufficient active sites, and poor selectivity for the desired reaction still require to be improved [19]. As far as we known, there is still a lack of reports on the room temperature gas sensing properties of BMO. This prompts people to study the gas sensing properties of BMO at RT further, especially at the acceptable level of various gas detection. To overcome this drawback, doping and forming heterojunctions are currently adopted to change or enhance their activity [20,21].Two-dimensional (2D) graphene nanosheets (GNs), as a zero band gap semiconductor, displays a remarkably high electron mobility under ambient conditions, suggesting that GNs in composites as the electron carrier may perform better as gas sensors. Some researchers have adopted the reduction graphene oxide (rGO) as substrate to enhance the electronic transmission of composites [22,23]. However, the functionalization of graphene oxide (GO) disrupts the electronic structure of graphene. Although the modified groups can be removed by reduction, it leaves a large number of defects, which affect the conductivity of graphene seriously [24]. Expanded graphene (EG), an economical carbon material with a honeycomb lattice, has triggered great research interest in serving as an ideal substrate for growing or loading functional materials due to its high mechanical strength and electrical conductivity [25]. Instead of functionalized GO, EG has the advantages of more excellent conductivity and layered structure of graphite. It has been proved that GNs from EG indeed can improve the electrical conductivities of composites, thereby resulting in enhanced sensor performance [26]. In this project, we attempted to combine the advantages of EG with Bi2MoO6 to develop a novel room temperature NO2 sensor. Herein, the EG was obtained through microwave expansion method by adopting commercial expandable graphite as raw material. The intercalated Bi2MoO6/graphene nanosheet composites (BMO/GNCs) were synthesized using EG as carrier, bismuth nitrate ((Bi(NO3)2•5H2O) and sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4•2H2O) as precursors, glycol and anhydrous ethanol as solvent, respectively. The formation strategy of BMO/GNCs is shown in Scheme 1 . Typically, both Bi(NO3)2 and Na2MoO4•2H2O are firstly pumped into the interlayers of EG through vacuum-assistant strategy, forming the interlayer precursor. In the subsequent solvothermal process, EG was reduced to few layer graphene by solvent, meanwhile, BMO NPs were in situ synthesized in the interlayers of GNs with high dispersion. Simultaneously, the force of Bi2MoO6 nanocrystalline growth enable the exfoliation of EG into thinner graphene sheets was realized. Also, the aggregation and packing of GNs are efficiently restricted by crystalline growth due to the reduced Van Edward force between GNs layers. As a consequence, the synthesized BMO/GNCs exhibit the significantly excellent gas-sensing properties to 100 ppm NO2 at room temperature with response rate of 96.73, which is 6.41 times of pure BMO NPs. The detection limit is 10 ppb, far exceeding the detection limit in commercial sensors. Therefore, the intercalated BMO/GNCs synthesized via vacuum-assistant and solvothermal strategy would be a promising gas sensor for the application in NO2 room temperature gas detection. The high gas-sensing properties of BMO/GNCs is mainly resulted from the following aspects: (i) The conduction band electron potential of Bi2MoO6 was optimized by forming BMO/GNCs with uniform BMO NPs intercalated in the interlayers of GNs; (ii) The transformation of electronic state on Mo atom during gas sensor process provides the efficient electron resource for NO2 oxidation reaction; (iii) GNs afford the highly effective electronic transmission channel for gas reaction process, which results to the quick gas response. Furthermore, And the intercalated BMO/GNCs synthesized by a simple method has good application potential in commercial gas sensors.

All reagents were analytical grade. The expansible graphite powder was purchased from Qingdao Graphite Factory. Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)2•5H2O) and sodium molybdate dehydrate (Na2MoO4•2H2O) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.

In our experiment, the expandable graphite powder (300 mesh) of 80 mg was placed in a 100 mL crucible, and then the powder was put in a microwave oven to heat by adjusting the expansion time. Finally, EG was obtained.

Intercalated BMO/GNCs were synthesized via a solvothermal chemical synthesis method. Bi(NO3)2•5H2O (0.8 mmol) and Na2MoO4•H2O (0.4 mmol) were added to 5 mL ethylene glycol under magnetic stirring, respectively. 30 mL ethanol was slowly added into the mixed solution, followed by 2 h stir. Subsequently, the as-prepared EG (80 mg) was placed in a suction bottle, and then sealed the bottle with a dropping funnel containing about 30 mL of the above mixed solution. After the suction bottle had been exposed in under vacuum for 0.5 h, the mixed solution was dropwise added so that the expanded structure would aid full infusion into the graphite layers. The precursor solution was transferred to the 50 mL stainless steel autoclave, then, respectively heated to 150, 160 or 180 °C for 12 h. After cooled to RT, the obtained nanocomposites were washed with deionized water and ethanol, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The nanocomposites were named as BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180. Other samples were prepared by adjusting the amount EG and temperature, and were named as pure BMO, BMO/GNCs-6-160, BMO/GNCs-10-160, BMO/GNCs-8-150 and BMO/GNCs-8-180 respectively. The detailed experimental conditions are listed in Table S2.

The crystal structure of the sample was identified by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D/MAX IIB-40KV, Japan, Cu–K radiation, = 1.5406 Å). The morphology was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHIS-48) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with an FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the sample was measured by N2 adsorption desorption (TriStar II3020). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy by a VGESCALAB MK II using Mg-Ka (1253.6 eV) achromatic X-ray radiation. BET surface area were measured by N2 adsorption-desorption (TriStar II3020). TheDRIFTS analysis in situ were conducted by using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer, containing high sensitive MCT detector with a DRIFTS cells (Harrick). Temperature program desorption (TPD) studies of NO2 were carried out by AutoChem TP5080 chemisorption analyzer, and using mass spectra (QIC-20, Hidden) to record the signals of NO2.

The 0.5 mg BMO/GNCs were dispersed uniformly in 0.5 mL ethanol to obtain suspension. Then, 0.05 mL suspension was dripped on the interdigital gold electrode (7 mm × 5 mm × 0.25 mm), which contains 100 fingers, and the distance between the two fingers is about 20 μm. Finally the BMO/GNCs film sensor was obtained with 7 h drying at 60 °C. The formation process of gas sensing thin films is shown in of Scheme S1 (Support Information).

The sensor was installed in the test chamber with inlet and outlet. The resistance of the sensor was measured at RT and relative humidity (RH, about 25%). The volume of gas was injected to control the gas concentration, and the chamber was purged by air to restore the sensor resistance. The gas sensing performance of sensors were measured on a static system at 22 °C. The response (S=Ra/Rg, (Rg < Ra)) value was calculated according to the formula reported [27–29], Where Rg and Ra are the thin film resistance, measured in NO2 and air atmosphere, respectively. The response and recovery time were defined as the time taken by the sensor to achieve 90% of the total resistance variation when adsorption and desorption, respectively [30]. The repeatability was studied by using four sensors under each sensor detection.

BMO/GNCs were prepared via the vacuum-assistant and one-step solvothermal method. Scheme 1 shows the preparation process of BMO/GNCs. First, Bi3+ and MoO4 2− precursor solution was pumped into the interlayers of EG by vacuum-assistant method. Then, free Bi3+ ions are formed when Bi(NO3)2•5H2O is completely dissolved in ethylene glycol and ethanol. The ethylene glycol interacted with the group on the surface of the EG by the hydrogen bond (with the O atom), meanwhile connected with Bi3+ ions [31]. As shown in Scheme 1, Bi3+ ions attracted MoO4 2− with electrostatic interaction. After solvothermal at 160 (or 150, 180) oC for 12 h. The reactions might occur during the synthetic process as follows [32]:
(1) 2Bi3+ + 2H2O → Bi2O2 2+ + 4H+
(2) Bi2O2 2+ + MoO4 2− → Mo2MoO6

Subsequently, BMO NPs were highly dispersed into the interlayers of EG in situ (Scheme 1). The nucleus of MoO4 2− grows rapidly in the precursor solution at low temperature [33], while the crystal growth of Bi2O2 2+ is slow [34]. For BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160, BMO/GNCs-8-180, the molar ratio of Bi:Mo (in the as synthesized Bi2MoO6) increases from 1.51:1, 1.57:1 to 1.78:1, increasing with solvothermal temperature as shown in Table S2 and Fig. S1.

This implied that the nucleation speed of Bi2O2 2+ and MoO4 2− is almost the same when the solvothermal temperature is 180 °C. It is to know that the relative chemical potential determines the morphology of the nanostructure. Therefore, according to Gibbs Thomson’s theory [35], the final morphology of crystal material depends largely on the average value of each atom’s suspended bond in the whole crystal [36]. It is determined by the inherent crystal structure (Scheme S2). The orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 NPs is constructed as an alternate Bi2O2 2+ sandwiched layers between the MoO4 2− (corner-shared MoO6 octahedron) layers shared by the corner.

The XRD diffraction patterns were primarily adopted to investigate the crystal structure of samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S2, the distinct diffraction peaks located at 10.8, 28.2, 32.5, 46.7, 55.5, 58.4 and 75.9°correspond respectively to the (020), (131), (200), (202), (133), (262) and (193) crystal planes of the identical orthorhombic phase of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS 76–2388), indicating the successful formation of Bi2MoO6 NPs for BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180 samples. The characteristic diffraction peak at 26.8° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphite (JCPDS 89–7213). In addition, the XRD diffraction patterns of BMO/GNCs-6-160 and BMO/GNCs-10-160 samples are displayed in Fig. S2. It was found that the diffraction peaks intensity corresponding to Bi2MoO6 are distinctly higher than that for BMO/GNCs-8-160, implying the Bi2MoO6 NPs in BMO/GNCs-8-160 sample should be in better crystallinity on GNs compared with BMO/GNCs-6-160 and BMO/GNCs-10-160. This result reveals that the EG content and solvothermal temperature have played the important role on the growth and crystallinity of Bi2MoO6 NPs. Besides, there are no other impurity peaks were detected apart from the peaks of Bi2MoO6 and GNs, indicating the high purity of the BMO/GNCs samples.

Subsequently, the pore size distribution and specific surface area of the nanocomposites were investigated by N2 adsorption-desorption technique. Fig. 1b shows that the isotherms of BMO and BMO/GNCs-8-160, which exhibited a type IV isotherm with a H1 type hysteresis loop according to the IUPAC classification. And the inset of Fig. 1b–d shows that the pore sizes of BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180 are 10.57, 20.35 and 16.14 nm, respectively. The BET isotherms for other BMO/GNCs are shown in Fig. 1b and d that all exhibit the typical mesoporous structures with the uniform pore size distribution. These abundant mesoporous structures can be resulted from the expanded space due to the intercalation of Bi2MoO6 NPs into GNs. The SEM and TEM images of all the samples exhibited in Fig. 2 and Fig. S3 further provided the direct evidence, revealing the porous structure. In addition, the specific surface area of composites can also be obtained by the BET analysis with the value was 41.59, 45.25, 46.20, 45.75, 43.3 and 41.54 m2g-1 for BMO, BMO/GNCs-6-160, BMO/GNCs-8-160, BMO/GNCs-10-160, BMO/GNCs-8-150 and BMO/GNCs-8-180, respectively. Among them, the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sample shows the highest specific surface area.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM and TEM images of BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180. For BMO/GNCs-8-150 (Fig. 2(a-a2)), one can see that BMO dispersed unevenly at 150 °C and BMO crystal grew little on GNs. For BMO/GNCs-8-180 (Fig. 2(c-c2)), the crystal growth of BMO speed quickly at 180 °C compared with BMO/GNCs-8-150 and BMO/GNCs-8-160, that is BMO displayed uniformly, and aggregated irregularly (Fig. 2c2). The SEM of different EG contents, the EDS and elemental analysis of BMO/GNCs-8-160 are shown in Fig. S3. As shown in Figs. S3d–f, the GNs of BMO/GNCs-8-160 is thinner than those of BMO/GNCs-8-150 and BMO/GNCs-8-180. The reason might be that BMO can grow well into the interlayers of EG at 160 °C, and EG was exfoliated with the growth of BMO in situ, resulting in separation between layers. In addition, the crystallinity of BMO increases with increasing temperature (Fig. 2(c-c2)), BMO appears to aggregate, and some agglomeration occurred at 180 °C.

The composition of BMO/GNCs-8-160 was further determined via EDS and elemental analysis. Fig. S3 (j, k) show the SEM image of BMO/GNCs-8-160 and the corresponding EDS, in which the atomic ratios of Bi, Mo, C was calculated to be 1.57:1:14.04 (Table S2). In Figs. S3(l–o), the element mapping was measured to further confirm their distribution, and all the elements including C, Bi, O and Mo were detected, indicating that both GNs and BMO do coexist and all of elements were well dispersed in the composites. This result provide the evidence that the synthesis of BMO/GNCs is a structurally homogeneous interaction, but not a simply physical mixture.

TEM and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were conducted for further investigating the morphology and crystal structure of BMO/GNCs. Fig. 3 a and b shows the typical TEM images of BMO/GNCs-8-160. It was found that the BMO NPs were dispersed uniformly on both surface and interlayer of GNs. The HRTEM images of BMO/GNCs-8-160, BMO/GNCs-8-150 and BMO/GNCs-8-180 are shown in Fig. 3c–e, Fig. S4e and Fig. S5 (c-e), respectively. Three types of lattice fringes are clearly visible at 0.31 (∼0.32), 0.28 and 0.19 (∼0.20) nm, which correspond to (131), (200) and (202) crystal planes of BMO, respectively. The lattice fringes of 0.34 (∼0.45), 0.20 nm correspond to (002) and (101) crystal planes of graphite. It can be found that the lattice spacing for GNs (002) is 0.45 nm, which is much larger than that for the standard graphite (002) crystal plane (0.333 nm). It indicates that the EG have been expanded during the hydrothermal process (Figs. S4(c–d)). Meanwhile, EG was simultaneously exfoliated during the solvothermal process with the interplanar spacing expanded to 0.89 nm (Fig. S4c), and the in situ growth of BMO NPs into the interlayers of EG was realized (Fig. S4d).The heterostructures were formed on the interface between GNs (101) and BMO (131) (Fig. 3c-e, Fig. S5e), GNs (002) and BMO (131) (Fig. S4e). The size of BMO NPs is approximately 10–30 nm in BMO/GNCs-8-150 (Fig. S4b). These interspaces enable the adsorption-desorption and diffusion of gas molecules more available. The red arrows in Fig. 3, Fig. S4 and Fig. S5 indicate that there are many linear and planar defects in the interface between the BMO NPs and GNs, which provide more active sites to adsorption gas molecules and transfer electrons [37]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180 are shown in Fig. 3f and Fig. S5f. The rings marked as black dash line are indexed (002) of GNs, similarly, from inside to outside the indexed (200), (202), (133) and (193) indicated BMO, respectively.

The XPS spectra of BMO and BMO/GNCs-8-160 were performed to further study the surface elements and chemical state of the composites. The BMO/GNCs-8-160 after NO2 gas sensor tests at room temperature for 2 h (named as BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2) was also analyzed for a comparison. Fig. 4 a shows the XPS spectra of BMO, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2. It is obvious that entire elements including N, C, Bi, Mo and O are detected in the XPS spectra, which is consistent with the result of EDS. The N1s peaks at 406.45, 407.75 and 407.90 eV correspond to NO3 − and NO2 − [38], as shown in Fig. 4b.

The C1s peak for BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2 are shown in Fig. 4c. When compared with the fresh BMO/GNCs-8-160, the C1s peak for BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2 shifted from 284.69 eV to higher binding energy (284.80 eV) because NO2 obtains electrons from GNs (Scheme 2 b). Focusing the C1s peaks for BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2, the broad peaks at 284.80 eV and 284.69 eV can be deconvolved into four peaks. Among them, the three C1s peaks at 284.80, 285.66, and 288.40 eV are assigned to C–C bonds with sp2 orbits, C–O bonds and C=O bonds, respectively [39–44]. In addition, the peak at 286.50 eV with dotted arrow in Fig. 4c is attributed to the Mo–O–C (Fig. 4e) bond and Bi–O–C (Fig. 4d) bonds formed between BMO and GNs [45–47]. These results demonstrate that the metal-O-C bonds are formed at the interface between BMO NPs and GNs in nanocomposite, that is, a large number of heterostructures are formed between BMO NPs and GNs.

For BMO, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2, the Bi 4f peaks (Fig. 4d) at 164.10 and 158.80 eV for BMO are ascribed to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of [Bi2O2]2+ [45]. Compared with BMO, the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 peaks of the fresh BMO/GNCs-8-160 shifted to higher energy by about 0.72, 0.72 eV (in Fig. 4d). In addition, the other two peaks at 165.40 and 160.10 eV corresponding to Bi–O–C were also detected [48]. These results are consistent with those of C 1s and further confirm the strong interaction between BMO NPs and GNs NSs. And compared with the fresh BMO/GNCs-8-160, the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 peaks of the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2 shifted to lower energy by approximate 0.42, 0.32 eV, respectively (in Fig. 4d). Moreover, Fig. 4e shows the Mo 3d XPS spectra of BMO, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2. For BMO, the peaks at 235.30 and 232.16 eV are assigned to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 of MoO4 2−. It can be deconvoluted into two peaks, respectively, therein, 231.30 eV and 234.09 eV are attributed to be Mo4+, 235.30 eV and 232.16 eV are Mo6+ [12,49], For the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2, the peak area percentages of Mo4+ and Mo6+ are 7.55% and 92.50%, respectively (Table S3), the amount of Mo4+ was lower than pure BMO and fresh BMO/GNCs-8-160. For the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2, two peaks at 236.21 and 232.96 eV shifted toward the higher binding energy, the Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 peaks of the used and fresh BMO/GNCs-8-160 positively shifted about +0.91, +0.8, and +0.14, +0.56 eV compared with pure BMO. Therefore, one can deduced from Fig. 4d and e, when NO2 adsorped on the BMO/GNCs-8-160, the possible electrons transfer from MoO4 2−(Mo4+) [50] to Bi2O2 2+ (via alternate sandwiched layers of BMO) and GNs (via Mo–O–C heterojunctions), thus, NO2 molecule possibly capture electrons on the surface of Bi3+ and GNs when NO2 detected. This demonstrated that heterojunctions were formed between BMO NPs and GNs in BMO/GNCs-8-160, which was responsible for the enhancement of gas sensing.

Furthermore, the O1s XPS spectrum and various oxygen species of BMO, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2 are shown in Fig. 4f and Table S4. For BMO, the O1s peak of samples can be fitted to three peaks, the binding energy was 531.53 (O3), 530.60 (O2) and 529.90 eV (O1) [51]. The peak of 529.90 eV (O1) is considered to be oxygen deficiency, 530.6 eV (O2) can be assigned to BMO lattice oxygen (Mo–O and Bi–O) bonds [52,53], and the peak of 531.53 eV (O3) is the typical surface adsorbed oxygen species (O2 −) on the BMO [54]. Similarly, O1s of BMO/GNCs-8-160 can be divided into three peaks, of which 531.35 eV (O2) is considered to be the lattice oxygen. In addition, the other two peaks (530.80 (O1) and 532.20 eV (O3)) are the oxygen deficiency and adsorbed oxygen species. As listed in Table S4, the adsorption oxygen species of the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2 increased significantly. The adsorbed oxygen on the surface can react with gas molecules, thus, they play a positive role in enhancing the gas sensing properties.

In general, the formation of the heterojunctions between active species and graphitic carbon could improve the electronic transmission and thus improve the gas sensing properties. In this study, BMO/GNCs with the Bi2MoO6 and GNs heterojunctions were adopted as gas sensors materials, which detected NO2 gas in the presence of RH = 25% at the RT. The gas sensing results are shown in Fig. 5 , Fig. S6, Fig. S7 and Table S5.

Fig. 5a shows the response and recovery time of the gas sensor exposed to different concentrations of NO2 at RT in air, which was evaluated on BMO/GNCs-8-160. Obviously, the response value is highly dependent on the gas concentration. A sharp decrease in surficial resistance of sensor has been detected with the introduction of NO2. Then a dramatic rise to its initial value was found after the sensor was re-exposed to air. During the detection of gas sensing, the oxidation-reduction reaction might occur bewtween NO2 and the adsorption of oxygen (O2 −) (Scheme 2b). When the p-type semiconductor is exposed to the NO2 gas molecule, the hole carrier density on the surface of the semiconductor increases owing to the loss of the electrons. These electron were captured by NO2 because of its high electron affinity, and thus the resistance of the semiconductor layer decreases (Fig. 5a). It can be deduced from Fig. 5a that BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor displays the typical conductive behavior of a p-type semiconductor. The gas response and response time of the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor to 100 ppm NO2 at RT were 96.73 and 2.51 s, respectively, with a detection limit of 10 ppb and a response of 1.51 (Fig. S6 shows the response and recovery time of BMO sensor exposed to different concentrations of NO2 at RT in air). Detailed results of gas response and response time of the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor are shown in Fig. 5b and Table S5.

As shown in Fig. 5c, the response of the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor has a good linearity (y = 0.5x+1.05, R2 = 0.9909). In the calibration curve, the relationship about the gas sensing response and NO2 concentration can be obtained, and the unknown concentration of NO2 gas can be identified by comparing its response value and reference curve.

Repeatability is an important parameter for judging the performance of sensors. Therefore, we tested the repeatability of the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor at RT, and the sensor exposed to 50 ppm NO2 was tested for 9 times. As shown in Fig. 5d, the resistance of the sensor shows a significant response. When NO2 is purged by air, the resistance return to the initial state quickly, and it is notable that the sensor still had the same response with no significant drop after multiple NO2 absorption and desorption cycles. The excellent gas sensing performance may be due to the large number of defects structure and heterojunctions (in nanocomposite) formed by the tightly contacts or strong interaction between BMO NPs and the GNs.

Fig. 5e-f and Table S5 display the histogram of the gas sensing performance on BMO/GNCs-6-160, BMO/GNCs-8-160, BMO/GNCs-10-160, BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-180 and BMO sensors. It can be clearly seen that the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor has the most outstanding gas sensing performance. The maximum response to 100 ppm NO2 is 96.73, which is 6.4 times to pure BMO sensor. To the best of our knowledge, the intercalated BMO/GNCs based sensors for a ppb-level NO2 are rarely reported. As shown in Table S6, the most of graphene electronically transmitted NO2 sensors have been tested at high temperatures in numerous reported work. Nevertheless, as-prepared BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor adopting GNs as the electron transport path, has an outstanding NO2 gas sensitivity performance at RT. Therefore, the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor may be more suitable for commercial application of NO2 sensing detection. Therefore, the BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor is suitable for commercial application of NO2 sensing detection.

Moreover, selectivity is a significant factor of gas sensor, the response of BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor to various gases (such as NH3, H2S, H2, CO, CH4) were studied at RT. In Fig. S7a, BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor shows the highest response and NO2 selectivity at RT, and have lower response to 500 ppm NH3, 300 ppm H2S and H2, 100 ppm CO, 200 ppm CH4 at RT, respectively. This indicates that BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor have low activity to reducing gases. In addition, from a practical perspective, long-term stability is one of the most important parameter of gas sensors. Therefore, in this work, the response of the BMO/GNCs-6-160 thin film sensor was constantly used to monitor its stability to 50 ppm NO2 at RT (Fig. S7b). This monitoring on the response have continued for 35 days (tested every 5 days) at RT (RH 25%). The sensor kept a good long-term stability in the whole 35 days.

The adsorption/desorption of NO2 on the surface of BMO/GNCs were further confirmed by NO2-TPD and DRIFT in situ. And the NO2-TPD results for nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 6 a. For BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180, the desorption peaks centered at 233 °C (246 °C, 249 °C) are similar. These higher temperature peak are attributed to the decomposition of highly thermally stable nitrates [55], which may be monodentate and nitric acid. Thus, several different nitrite and nitrate species are formed on the surface of three nanocomposites. For BMO/GNCs-8-160, the peak of lower temperature around 76 °C is the decomposition of nitrite or unstable nitrate species, which may be linear and bridged nitrite [56]. The NO2 desorption peaks of BMO/GNCs-8-160 at which appear at lower temperature (76 °C) and higher temperature (233 °C) are large compared with BMO/GNCs-8-150 and BMO/GNCs-8-180, this indicated that the amount of adsorbed NO2 is also the most and easiest, BMO/GNCs-8-160 exhibits the higher adsorption-desorption capacity, so, this may be related to the high gas sensing performance of BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor. The oxygen desorption behavior of BMO/GNCs were further studied by using O2-TPD. As shown in Fig. 6b, BMO/GNCs-8-150, BMO/GNCs-8-160 and BMO/GNCs-8-180 show different oxygen peaks at 154 (155, 158), 333 (361, 362), 381(454, 473), 501 (520, 562) °C, respectively. Usually, in the O2-TPD curve, the desorbed oxygen exists in the following species: O2ad, O2 − (chem), O− (ad) and O2− (lattice) [57]. Physically adsorbed oxygen (O2ad) and chemically adsorbed oxygen (O2 − (ad)/O− (chem)) species are much easier to desorb than the O2− (lattice) species [58]. Therefore, at very low temperatures (<250 °C), desorption can be assigned to physically adsorbed oxygen, and the desorption peaks between 250 and 500 °C can be attributed to the types of chemically adsorbed oxygen. In addition, the desorption peak at high temperature (>500 °C) can be attributed to bulk lattice oxygen [59]. Therefore, in the case of BMO/GNCs-8-160, the intensity of desorption peak of O2ad and O2 − (chem) species is the most among nanocomposites.

DRIFT spectra of adsorbed NO2 on BMO/GNCs-8-160 is shown in Fig. 6c. Several adsorbed NO2 species bands appeared in the DRIFT spectrum at 1–20 min. It was observed that the bands are related to linear nitrite (1425 cm−1), bridged nitrate (∼1435 cm−1) and monodentate nitrate (∼1506 cm−1). These bands are unstable and then disappear after 20 min. The band at 1600 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 may be related to the double-dentate nitrate and nitro and nitrate species [60–62]. It was observed that the area of the two peaks of 1627 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 hardly changed from 1 to 20 min. This may be related to the excellent performance and fast response of BMO/GNCs-8-160 at 30 °C. In Fig. 6d, the results of the N2 purge treatment at different times at 30 °C are shown, and it is clear that almost all of the adsorbed NO2 (nitro and nitrate species) were removed for the 10 min. This may be related to the very short recovery time of BMO/GNCs-8-160 at RT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas sensing mechanism of the BMO/GNCs sensors
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that gas sensing is a surface-related reaction process, the most popular and widely accepted gas sensing principle is the adsorption/desorption of gas molecules based on the change of sensor’s resistance [27]. In this work, the enhanced gas sensing performance of the intercalated BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor to NO2 can be attributed to three factors: (1) As a substrate, the GNs not only enhances the conductivity of the sensor, but also accelerate electron transfer (Scheme 2b), which leads electrons can be quickly transferred to its surface via the high-speed electron channel [63,64].

In addition, the synergistic effect between BMO NPs and GNs could creat the p-p junctions, which acts as a low-speed electron transfer channels; the advantage of the synergy between BMO and GNs which possesses superior conductivity benefits the sensing of the intercalated BMO/GNCs by providing affluent electrons; (2) The unique structure of nanocomposite: the extensive interspaces in the GNs would provide a gas adsorption/desorption and diffusion channel and thus finally advance the gas diffusion speed tothe target gas; (3) The abundant interfaces between BMO NPs and GNs that in particular in BMO/GNCs-8-160, herein, oxygen adsorption plays an important role in gas sensing, because the chemisorption oxygen is considered to be an electron donor [65], the formation of many known oxygen species (O2 −) has good catalytic activity in gas sensors, as well as improves the performance of NO2 sensors. The gas sensing mechanism is shown in Scheme 2.

As shown in Scheme 2 (a, b c), when BMO/GNCs are exposed to air, oxygen molecules can easily capture electrons by capturing free electrons to form oxygen ions or chemisorbed oxygen (O2 −) [66]. When the BMO/GNCs based sensor is exposed to NO2, the gas molecules can attract electrons from the BMO/GNCs due to the high electron affinity of NO2 molecule, resulting in electrons migrate from nanocomposites to NO2. The adsorption of NO2 on BMO/GNCs leads to NO2 − (Scheme 2b), this results in an increase in hole density, the result is a rapid decrease in resistance (Scheme 2b). Also, the target gas molecules (NO2) can directly adsorb electrons from the BMO/GNCs, then react with O2 − to form NO3 − in situ (Scheme 2(c, d)). As shown in Scheme 2d, the strong band at 1384 and 2395 cm−1 of the BMO/GNCs-8-160+NO2 is attributed to the symmetric stretching band of NO3 − [67], indicating that NO2 is fully adsorbed into the BMO/GNCs-8-160, and captured an electron, then reacted with O− and O2 −(see Scheme 2b reaction (3)).

The I–V curve in Scheme 2e further confirms the NO2 gas reaction on the surface, notably the decrease in current during the adsorption of NO2 molecules [68,69]. Due to the oxidizing nature of NO2, it is easily adsorbed on the surface of the BMO/GNCs-8-160 and extracts electrons from the nanocomposites. This results in a decrease in the electron carrier density in BMO/GNCs-8-160, and a significant change in electrical resistance, which demonstrates the high sensitivity of the sensor to NO2 gas at RT.

Intercalated BMO/GNCs were successfully synthesized by vacuum-assistant and solvothermal strategy. The BMO/GNCs-8-160 with the well-dispersed BMO NPs, a large number of interface structures defects/chemisorbed oxygen and p-p junctions provide many electronic transmission channels/active sites, and many expanded interspaces between the interlayers of GNs as a gas diffusion channel might also increase the adsorption/desorption or diffusion speed of the target gas. As a result, the synergy of the superior conductive GNs and BMO NPs allows for the outstanding NO2 gas sensing performance, and the optimized BMO/GNCs-8-160 sensor has excellent response, low detection limit (10 ppb) for NO2 at RT, and has excellent selectivity, great stability and reproducibility. This strategy combines the BMO nanoparticles with the high conductive graphene nanosheets to construct an intercalated heterostructure that will develope a new opportunity for the application of room temperature-type sensors.
